title: Simon from Cyrene part 1
scripture_text: ‘’A certain man from Cyrene, Simon, the father of Alexander and Rufus, was passing by on his way in from the country, and they forced him to carry the cross.’’
devotional:
The record for most extras in a film is in the 1982 classic Gandhi, which used

over 300,000 extras for the funeral scene. About 200,000 were volunteers and

94,560 were paid a small fee (under contract). The sequence was filmed on 31st

Jan 1981, the 33rd anniversary of Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi's funeral. 11 crews shot

over 20,000 feet of film, which was pared down to 125 seconds in the final

release.

In the massive drama of the passion of the cross it could be very easy to miss

the role played by others who could be perceived as ‘extras,’ seen as those who

have blended into the background, with very little significance to the plot line,

Simon of Cyrene could be viewed in this way. We know that he was the man who

helped Jesus carry his cross. He is known and unknown. He is there but rarely

seen. Why would three of the gospels mention him? Matthew 27:32, Mark

15:21 and Luke 23:26;. He was from Cyrene, modern day Libya, about 900 miles

from Jerusalem. Cyrene had a large Jewish population, it may be that Simon had

travelled to Jerusalem for the passover. Two other things to mention. He had

two sons Alexander and Rufus who were known to the early church, see Romans

16:13. And Luke tells us that Simon was going into the city as Jesus was about

to leave it, and it was then when he was forced to carry the cross of Jesus. Although

Simon never says a word in scripture this incident in his life has a lot to say to

us.

Simon was someone who shared in the Suffering of Jesus, he was the only

person who helped Jesus on this terrible day. To share in Christ’s sufferings was

one of the Apostle Paul’s aspirations when he said ’I want to know Christ—yes,

to know the power of his resurrection and participation in his sufferings, becoming like him in his death. ’To participate in the suffering of Jesus is ‘fellowship of the heart,’ it is the deepest kind of relationship anyone can have with Jesus, it is the sharing the feeling of sorrow that happens when we choose to share his cross. At the place of suffering there is also the simultaneous call to intimacy.

So what does it mean to share in his suffering?

We need to accept the unexpected burdens that are forced upon us. In those days a Roman solider had the right commandeer any civilian to carry a burden like their kit, or the patibulum the cross-bar of the cross that usually weighed between thirty or forty pounds and was usually strapped across the shoulders. No Roman soldier would disgrace himself by carrying a cross for a criminal, so they forced Simon to carry the cross. This was not something that Simon was expecting, or anticipated, he was a passer-by when he was forced into the drama of the cross.

Often in life unexpected burdens come our way, burdens that draw us into dramas that we don’t fully understand. We find ourselves in circumstances that are beyond our control, our freedom is taken from us, our plans are lying in ruins. We could feel a bit like Simon, embracing something that feels cruel and reeking of death. Maybe this is the only way we have of sharing in the fellowship of Jesus’ suffering. We will never have to re-enact what Simon did on Good Friday, but at some point in our lives, the burdens that are enforced upon us will cause us to decide if we are really prepared to endure the burden, and pay the price of following Jesus to the extent that we can have fellowship in his suffering. Like Simon we have no control or say in what burden we may have to carry, however, we can choose to allow those burdens to direct us in the way of Jesus. The unexpected crisis, burdens and interruptions that disrupt our lives could be the things that make Jesus more real. They could be the means whereby we can follow Jesus on a path that we would never choose for ourselves.
prayer: Father may the unexpected burdens of life help me to identify with Jesus.
html:
Simon from Cyrene part 1

Study Text: Mark 15:21
‘’A certain man from Cyrene, Simon, the father of Alexander and Rufus, was passing by on his way in from the country, and they forced him to carry the cross.’’

The record for most extras in a film is in the 1982 classic Gandhi, which used

over 300,000 extras for the funeral scene. About 200,000 were volunteers and

94,560 were paid a small fee (under contract). The sequence was filmed on 31st

Jan 1981, the 33rd anniversary of Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi's funeral. 11 crews shot

over 20,000 feet of film, which was pared down to 125 seconds in the final

release.

In the massive drama of the passion of the cross it could be very easy to miss

the role played by others who could be perceived as ‘extras,’ seen as those who

have blended into the background, with very little significance to the plot line,

Simon of Cyrene could be viewed in this way. We know that he was the man who

helped Jesus carry his cross. He is known and unknown. He is there but rarely

seen. Why would three of the gospels mention him? Matthew 27:32, Mark

15:21 and Luke 23:26;. He was from Cyrene, modern day Libya, about 900 miles

from Jerusalem. Cyrene had a large Jewish population, it may be that Simon had

travelled to Jerusalem for the passover. Two other things to mention. He had

two sons Alexander and Rufus who were known to the early church, see Romans

16:13. And Luke tells us that Simon was going into the city as Jesus was about

to leave it, and it was then when he was forced to carry the cross of Jesus. Although

Simon never says a word in scripture this incident in his life has a lot to say to

us.

Simon was someone who shared in the Suffering of Jesus, he was the only

person who helped Jesus on this terrible day. To share in Christ’s sufferings was

one of the Apostle Paul’s aspirations when he said ’I want to know Christ—yes,

to know the power of his resurrection and participation in his sufferings, becoming like him in his death. ’To participate in the suffering of Jesus is ‘fellowship of the heart,’ it is the deepest kind of relationship anyone can have with Jesus, it is the sharing the feeling of sorrow that happens when we choose to share his cross. At the place of suffering there is also the simultaneous call to intimacy.

So what does it mean to share in his suffering?

We need to accept the unexpected burdens that are forced upon us. In those days a Roman solider had the right commandeer any civilian to carry a burden like their kit, or the patibulum the cross-bar of the cross that usually weighed between thirty or forty pounds and was usually strapped across the shoulders. No Roman soldier would disgrace himself by carrying a cross for a criminal, so they forced Simon to carry the cross. This was not something that Simon was expecting, or anticipated, he was a passer-by when he was forced into the drama of the cross.

Often in life unexpected burdens come our way, burdens that draw us into dramas that we don’t fully understand. We find ourselves in circumstances that are beyond our control, our freedom is taken from us, our plans are lying in ruins. We could feel a bit like Simon, embracing something that feels cruel and reeking of death. Maybe this is the only way we have of sharing in the fellowship of Jesus’ suffering. We will never have to re-enact what Simon did on Good Friday, but at some point in our lives, the burdens that are enforced upon us will cause us to decide if we are really prepared to endure the burden, and pay the price of following Jesus to the extent that we can have fellowship in his suffering. Like Simon we have no control or say in what burden we may have to carry, however, we can choose to allow those burdens to direct us in the way of Jesus. The unexpected crisis, burdens and interruptions that disrupt our lives could be the things that make Jesus more real. They could be the means whereby we can follow Jesus on a path that we would never choose for ourselves.

Prayer: Father may the unexpected burdens of life help me to identify with Jesus.
Bible in a year reading plan: - Psalms 6-9
